items.q: What does the dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia state?
items.correct: Positive symptoms are linked to excess dopamine activity in mesolimbic pathways.
items.misconception: Schizophrenia is caused by too little dopamine.
items.random_wrong: Schizophrenia is caused by too much serotonin.
items.why: Students reverse the direction. The original hypothesis was HYPERdopaminergia. The revised version notes hypoactivity in prefrontal areas for negative symptoms, but excess in mesolimbic for positive.

items.q: Which component handles visual and spatial information?
items.why: Students mix up the slave systems. The phonological loop is for auditory/verbal information; the sketchpad is for visual/spatial. The central executive coordinates but doesn't store.

items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: Vygotsky's term for the gap between solo and supported ability is the:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Zone of proximal development. “Sensorimotor zone” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is the strongest AO3 criticism of Piaget?
items.why: Correct answer: He underestimated young children. “He invented the term schema” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Piaget's formal operational stage typically begins around age:
items.why: Exact answer: 11. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
